growth_records.medium: SN medium (Waterbury et al. recipe), coastal seawater base
growth_records.cite: Zhu et al. Characterization of phytoplankton-excreted metabolites mediating carbon flux through the surface ocean. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (2026). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2531765123. [PMC13012070]
growth_records.snippet: Synechococcus WH8102 ... SN ... 23 ... 45 (14:10) ... Exponential phase growth rate (d-1) 0.54
growth_records.notes: Table 1 reports exponential-phase growth rate = 0.54 d-1 directly (RFU-based, batch tube culture, 14:10 h light:dark, 45 umol photons m-2 s-1). Converted to per-hour: mu = 0.54/24 = 0.0225 h-1; doubling_time_h = ln2/0.0225 = 30.81 h.

growth_records.cite: Christie-Oleza et al. Nutrient recycling facilitates long-term stability of marine microbial phototroph-heterotroph interactions. Nature Microbiology (2017). DOI:10.1038/nmicrobiol.2017.100. [PMC5495174]
growth_records.snippet: No significant difference in growth rate or maximum cell densities was observed for Synechococcus sp. WH7803 when grown in the presence/absence of the heterotroph during exponential phase (38.2 and 38.7 h doubling times,
growth_records.notes: Axenic culture doubling time of 38.7 h explicitly reported for exponential phase growth in ASW (no heterotroph present). growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln2/38.7 = 0.0179 h^-1. Cultured in 100 ml ASW in 250 ml Erlenmeyer flasks at 22°C, 10 μmol photons m-2 s-1 light, 140 rpm shaking (Methods). ASW contained no vitamins; N and P supplied as stated unless noted otherwise.

growth_records.strain: WH7803 (co-culture with heterotroph, e.g. Ruegeria pomeroyi DSS-3)
growth_records.cite: Christie-Oleza et al. Nutrient recycling facilitates long-term stability of marine microbial phototroph-heterotroph interactions. Nature Microbiology (2017). DOI:10.1038/nmicrobiol.2017.100. [PMC5495174]
growth_records.snippet: No significant difference in growth rate or maximum cell densities was observed for Synechococcus sp. WH7803 when grown in the presence/absence of the heterotroph during exponential phase (38.2 and 38.7 h doubling times,
growth_records.notes: Co-culture (with heterotroph, e.g. R. pomeroyi DSS-3) doubling time of 38.2 h explicitly reported for exponential phase growth in ASW; not statistically different from axenic (38.7 h). growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln2/38.2 = 0.0181 h^-1. Same ASW medium/conditions as axenic control (22°C, 10 μmol photons m-2 s-1, 140 rpm).

growth_records.lvlwhy: the source paper (often a review/modeling reuse) states no strain
growth_records.medium: Natural seawater (North Pacific Ocean surface field samples); no defined culture medium — picocyanobacterial growth/mortality rates are field/in situ, with culture-based reference values cited to literature
growth_records.cite: Carlson et al. Viruses affect picocyanobacterial abundance and biogeography in the North Pacific Ocean. Nature Microbiology (2022). DOI:10.1038/s41564-022-01088-x. [PMC8975747]
growth_records.snippet: Synechococcus is expected to have faster growth rates at these temperatures, doubling every 1.1 ± 0.2 d (refs. 12,46).
growth_records.notes: This is a literature-derived assumption cited from refs. 12 and 46 (not a direct measurement by the present field study) used to model virus-induced mortality in the North Pacific transition zone ('these temperatures' = low water-column temperatures of the study region; exact °C not given). doubling_time_h = 1.1 d x 24 = 26.4 h; growth_rate_per_h = ln2/26.4 = 0.0263 h^-1. No defined culture medium (in situ marine population). [metadata completed from paper Methods: temperature, medium]

growth_records.medium: Artificial seawater (defined; NO3-:PO4^3- input ratio = 1.7, i.e. N-stress; full composition in Table S1)
growth_records.cite: Garcia et al. Proteome trait regulation of marine Synechococcus elemental stoichiometry under global change. ISME J (2024). DOI:10.1093/ismejo/wrae046. [PMC11020310]
growth_records.snippet: We used two concentration ratios of macronutrients (NO3-:PO43- = 1.7 and 80) and three levels of temperature (20, 24, and 28C)... We measured our fixed dilution rate across treatments at 0.178 +/- 0.004 (mean +/- SD) day-1
growth_records.notes: Chemostat at steady state so mu = D. Reported D = 0.178 day-1. mu(h-1) = 0.178/24 = 0.007417 h-1. Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.178 day = 3.894 day = 93.46 h. Condition: N-stress (NO3:PO4 input = 1.7) at 20C. Full artificial-seawater medium composition is in supplementary Table S1, not present in the fulltext XML, hence composition:[] and stated:false. No specific substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes (mmol/gDW/h) reported in fulltext; nutrient uptake could in principle be derived as q = D x cellular quota but numeric C/N/P quotas are given only in figures, not in extractable text, so not computed to avoid fabrication.

growth_records.medium: Artificial seawater (defined; NO3-:PO4^3- input ratio = 80, i.e. P-stress; full composition in Table S1)
growth_records.cite: Garcia et al. Proteome trait regulation of marine Synechococcus elemental stoichiometry under global change. ISME J (2024). DOI:10.1093/ismejo/wrae046. [PMC11020310]
growth_records.snippet: Our experimental design covered P-stress (N:P_input = 80:1) and N-stress (N:P_input = 1.7:1) at 20C, 24C, and 28C. We measured our fixed dilution rate across treatments at 0.178 +/- 0.004 (mean +/- SD) day-1
growth_records.notes: Chemostat steady state so mu = D. Fixed D = 0.178 day-1. mu(h-1) = 0.178/24 = 0.007417 h-1. td = ln2/0.178 day = 3.894 day = 93.46 h. Condition: P-stress (NO3:PO4 input = 80) at 20C. Full medium composition only in Table S1, absent from fulltext -> composition:[]/stated:false. No flux rates in extractable text.

sec.units: pmol CH4 per 10^6 cells per h (range 0.01-0.6)
sec.method: MIMS continuous measurement; slope of CH4 profile normalized to cell counts

growth_records.cite: Bižić M. et al. Aquatic and terrestrial cyanobacteria produce methane. Science Advances (2020). DOI:10.1126/sciadv.aax5343. [PMC6962044]
growth_records.snippet: Among the marine picophytoplankton, Synechococcus sp. exhibited low rates ranging between 0.01 and 0.6 pmol CH4 per 106 cells hour−1
growth_records.notes: CH4 secretion rate 0.01-0.6 pmol CH4 (10^6 cells)^-1 h^-1 (cell-count normalized, not gDW, because of small cell size). Aggregate across marine Synechococcus strains WH7803/WH8102; no per-strain value in text. Media Pro99/ASW cited by reference, composition not stated. Temperature 22°C (Table 1). No growth rate reported; CH4 requires light >20 µmol quanta m-2 s-1.

growth_records.medium: f/2 medium (Guillard and Ryther 1962) in filtered seawater, augmented with 12C/13C-bicarbonate; SIP calibration treatment f_media=0.32
growth_records.cite: Gordon T. Taylor et al. Single-Cell Growth Rates in Photoautotrophic Populations Measured by Stable Isotope Probing and Resonance Raman Microspectrometry. Frontiers in Microbiology (2017). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2017.01449. [PMC5541042]
growth_records.snippet: median growth rates computed over the entire exponential phase of f media 0.32 and 0.43 cultures were 0.22 +/- 0.07 and 0.20 +/- 0.07 day-1 for single cells (Raman-derived) and 0.22 +/- 0.07 and 0.22 +/- 0.07 day-1 for populations (fluoresc
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote = cyanobacterium Synechococcus sp. RS9916. f_media=0.32 treatment. Reported median growth rates over exponential phase (0-18 d): population (in vivo chlorophyll-a fluorescence, ln AFU vs time slope) = 0.22 +/- 0.07 day-1; single-cell (SCRR Raman) = 0.22 +/- 0.07 day-1. growth_rate_per_h converted from 0.22 day-1: 0.22/24 = 0.00917 h-1. doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.22 = 3.15 day = 75.6 h (paper states g ~ 3.3 days for these slow-growing photoautotrophs). Paper also reports overall mean mu_pop ~0.21 day-1 from 0-18 d for both 0.32 and 0.43 populations. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates reported (photoautotroph fixing dissolved inorganic 13C-bicarbonate; not quantified as mmol/gDW/h). This is a SIP/Raman single-cell method paper, not a 13C-MFA flux-partitioning study, so no mfa_fluxes.

growth_records.medium: f/2 medium (Guillard and Ryther 1962) in filtered seawater, augmented with 12C/13C-bicarbonate; SIP calibration treatment f_media=0.43
growth_records.cite: Gordon T. Taylor et al. Single-Cell Growth Rates in Photoautotrophic Populations Measured by Stable Isotope Probing and Resonance Raman Microspectrometry. Frontiers in Microbiology (2017). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2017.01449. [PMC5541042]
growth_records.snippet: median growth rates computed over the entire exponential phase of f media 0.32 and 0.43 cultures were 0.22 +/- 0.07 and 0.20 +/- 0.07 day-1 for single cells (Raman-derived) and 0.22 +/- 0.07 and 0.22 +/- 0.07 day-1 for populations (fluoresc
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote = cyanobacterium Synechococcus sp. RS9916. f_media=0.43 treatment. Reported median growth rates over exponential phase (0-18 d): population (in vivo chlorophyll-a fluorescence) = 0.22 +/- 0.07 day-1; single-cell (SCRR Raman) = 0.20 +/- 0.07 day-1. growth_rate_per_h converted from population 0.22 day-1: 0.22/24 = 0.00917 h-1 (single-cell 0.20 day-1 = 0.20/24 = 0.00833 h-1). doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.22 = 3.15 day = 75.6 h (paper states g ~ 3.3 days). Growth conditions deliberately held constant across f_media treatments (C_T fixed while f_media manipulated) so growth is essentially identical to the 0.32 record. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates reported (photoautotroph). Not a 13C-MFA flux study; no mfa_fluxes.

growth_records.medium: Modified SN medium (75% filtered seawater + 25% milli-Q), P-limited, N:P=441
growth_records.cite: Kretz, Cecilia B. et al. Influence of growth rate on the physiological response of marine Synechococcus to phosphate limitation. Frontiers in Microbiology (2015). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2015.00085. [PMC4324148]
growth_records.snippet: growth rate averaged to 0.2 day−1, 0.3 day−1, and 0.7 day−1 per strain ... growth rate was manipulated by changing the speed of the pump ... which in turn changed the dilution rate
growth_records.notes: Chemostat lowest setpoint ~0.2 day-1 for WH8102 (both replicate vessels 0.2/day, Table 1). At steady state mu=D. Converted to per-h: 0.2/24=0.00833 h-1. td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.00833=83.2 h. Phosphate is the limiting substrate but no biomass-specific P uptake rate (mmol/gDW/h) reported; only residual SRP (1.0-9.2 nM), Ks,p and cellular C:N:P given, so uptake_rates left empty (cannot derive gDW-based rate without biomass). Ks,p 0.8-2.5 nM and P drawdown c 0.9-1.2 nM for WH8102 (Table 1).

growth_records.medium: Modified SN medium (75% filtered seawater + 25% milli-Q), P-limited, N:P=441
growth_records.cite: Kretz, Cecilia B. et al. Influence of growth rate on the physiological response of marine Synechococcus to phosphate limitation. Frontiers in Microbiology (2015). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2015.00085. [PMC4324148]
growth_records.snippet: WH8102 replicate 1 T2=0.3 day−1, replicate 2 T2=0.4 day−1 (Table 1)
growth_records.notes: Chemostat intermediate setpoint for WH8102: replicate vessels gave 0.3 and 0.4 day-1 (Table 1); mean=0.35 day-1. mu=D at steady state; 0.35/24=0.0146 h-1. td=ln2/0.0146=47.5 h. Phosphate-limited; no gDW-based uptake rate reported.

growth_records.notes: Chemostat highest setpoint for CC9311: replicate vessels 0.7 and 0.8 day-1 (Table 1); mean=0.75 day-1. mu=D=0.75/24=0.03125 h-1. td=ln2/0.03125=22.2 h. P-limited; no gDW uptake rate reported.

growth_records.cite: Dufour et al. Competition for light color between marine Synechococcus strains with fixed and variable pigmentation. Applied and Environmental Microbiology (2025). DOI:10.1128/aem.00087-25. [PMC12366368]
growth_records.snippet: Continuous supply (41.7 uL min-1, corresponding to a dilution rate of 0.1 day-1) of PCR-S11 medium... continuously diluted until the culture reached the steady state, that is, at least five consecutive days with less than 10% variation in c
growth_records.notes: Photoautotrophic light-limited chemostat; no chemical substrate-uptake or product-secretion rates reported (light is the limiting resource; no mmol/gDW/h data). Growth rate CALCULATED as mu=D at steady state: D=0.1 day-1 = 0.1/24 = 0.004167 h-1. Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.1=6.931 days=6.931*24=166.4 h. Steady-state cell density ~3.6x10^7 cells/mL (Fig 2A). Critical transmitted light Iout for LBL specialist reported as 5.92+/-0.12 umol photons m-2 s-1 (light 'uptake' proxy, not a metabolic rate). Base PCR-S11 composition given only by reference 32; only the two stated supplements captured. Light: LBL = 15 umol photons m-2 s-1 blue LED, continuous light.

sec.method: HPLC of culture supernatant, linear regression of extracellular lysine (Table 2)

growth_records.cite: Korosh, Travis C. et al. Engineering photosynthetic production of L-lysine. Metabolic Engineering (2017). DOI:10.1016/j.ymben.2017.10.010. [PMC5776718]
growth_records.method: reported (volumetric linear growth rate, not specific mu)
growth_records.notes: Table 2 row. Reported linear growth rate 0.027 gDCW/L/h (VOLUMETRIC, light-limited linear phase, NOT specific mu; growth_rate_per_h left null). Lysine productivity 0.004 mM/h (text: 'AM.319 - 0.004 mM hr-1'; table header 'mmol lysine h-1' is per-liter i.e. mM/h). %C 2%, titer 0.78 mM. Media A+ composition cited (Jr et al 1973), not in body.

growth_records.cite: Korosh, Travis C. et al. Engineering photosynthetic production of L-lysine. Metabolic Engineering (2017). DOI:10.1016/j.ymben.2017.10.010. [PMC5776718]
growth_records.notes: Table 2, TK.032 nitrate media (24 mM NO3). Volumetric growth 0.013 gDCW/L/h, productivity 0.006 mM/h, 7%C, titer 1.14 mM. Lower growth than standard Media A+.

sec.method: reported qualitatively ('these cultures excreted other amino acids, Data Not Shown')

growth_records.cite: Korosh, Travis C. et al. Engineering photosynthetic production of L-lysine. Metabolic Engineering (2017). DOI:10.1016/j.ymben.2017.10.010. [PMC5776718]
growth_records.snippet: grown on ammonium media produced lysine at higher rates (0.020 mM hr-1) and sent the largest percentage of carbon to lysine (18%) ... TK.032a | ... | NH4 | 100 | 0.017 | 0.020 | 18 | 3.11
growth_records.notes: Table 2, TK.032 on ammonium (24 mM NH4). Volumetric growth 0.017 gDCW/L/h, productivity 0.020 mM/h, 18%C (highest), titer 3.11 mM. Reduced N source enhanced lysine via reducing power. Cultures also excreted other (unquantified) amino acids.

growth_records.cite: Korosh, Travis C. et al. Engineering photosynthetic production of L-lysine. Metabolic Engineering (2017). DOI:10.1016/j.ymben.2017.10.010. [PMC5776718]
growth_records.snippet: In GBF based media, TK.032 grew more slowly (0.014 g DCW L-1 hr-1) and directed only 3% of carbon to lysine resulting in low titer (0.28 mM) and volumetric productivity 0.003 mM hr-1.
growth_records.notes: Table 2, TK.032 in wastewater-derived GBF media at 27 C, 1000 ng/mL aTc. Volumetric growth 0.014 gDCW/L/h, productivity 0.003 mM/h, 3%C, titer 0.28 mM. Growth on non-traditional nutrient source.

growth_records.cite: Korosh, Travis C. et al. Engineering photosynthetic production of L-lysine. Metabolic Engineering (2017). DOI:10.1016/j.ymben.2017.10.010. [PMC5776718]
growth_records.method: reported (no growth after induction at 27 C)
growth_records.snippet: TK.032 | ybjE | AK | 27 | A+ | 1000 | 0 | nd | nd | nd (Table 2); after induction in Media A+ at this temperature, cultures were notably chlorotic
growth_records.notes: Table 2 negative-control condition: TK.032 in Media A+ at 27 C, 1000 ng/mL aTc -> growth rate 0 (chlorotic, growth-arrested after induction); productivity/titer 'nd'. Captured as a distinct condition with a measured null-growth result.

growth_records.medium: Media A (photobioreactor; +excess NaNO3, KH2PO4, FeCl3, CO2)

sec.method: HPLC of supernatant; mean linear L-lysine production = 13 +/- 0.5 uM/h over first 76 h linear phase, n=3

growth_records.cite: Korosh, Travis C. et al. Engineering photosynthetic production of L-lysine. Metabolic Engineering (2017). DOI:10.1016/j.ymben.2017.10.010. [PMC5776718]
growth_records.method: reported (mean linear growth rate over first 76 h of linear phase, n=3)
growth_records.snippet: the mean linear growth rate of TK.032 was 10(+/-0.4) mg DW L-1 hr-1 (S.E.) and the mean linear rate of L-lysine production was 13(+/-0.5) uM L-lysine hr-1 ... biomass concentration was 2.4(+/-0.1) g DW L-1 and the L-lysine titer was 2.7(+/-
growth_records.notes: Section 3.4 photobioreactor batch, n=3. Mean linear (volumetric) growth rate = 10 +/- 0.4 mg DW/L/h = 0.010 gDCW/L/h (light-limited linear phase, not specific mu -> growth_rate_per_h null). Mean linear lysine production 13 +/- 0.5 uM/h = 0.013 mM/h. 18% carbon flux to lysine. At stationary (20 days): biomass 2.4 +/- 0.1 gDW/L, lysine titer 2.7 +/- 0.3 mM. Discussion states highest volumetric lysine productivity 0.003 g/L/h. Reactor temperature not explicitly stated in body.

yields.product_yields: fatty acids 130 mg/L (Ruffing 2014); ethanol 0.25% v/v (Kopka et al. 2017) - final titers, not per-time rates

growth_records.cite: Agarwal et al. Cyanobacteria as a Promising Alternative for Sustainable Environment: Synthesis of Biofuel and Biodegradable Plastics. Frontiers in Microbiology (2022). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2022.939347. [PMC9325326]
growth_records.snippet: Synechococcus sp. PCC 7002 | Photoautotrophic | Agar plates of medium A+ | 0.2 h-1 | Fatty acids | 130 mg/L
growth_records.notes: SECONDARY value cited within REVIEW (Agarwal et al. 2022). Specific growth rate 0.2 h-1 -> doubling time=ln2/0.2=3.47 h. Original sources: Ruffing 2014 (medium A+, fatty acids), Kopka et al. 2017 (CO2-enriched seawater, ethanol). Product values are titers, not rates. Temp/pH not stated.

up.method: 2% CO2 sparge; photoautotrophic fixation; rate not quantified numerically

sec.method: net volumetric O2 evolution measured with Clark O2 electrode; increases linearly with growth rate (Figure 1B) but numeric values only shown graphically, not tabulated

growth_records.cite: Bernstein, Hans C. et al. Effect of mono- and dichromatic light quality on growth rates and photosynthetic performance of Synechococcus sp. PCC 7002. Frontiers in Microbiology (2014). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2014.00488. [PMC4168726]
growth_records.snippet: Rapid specific growth rates (mu) of up to 0.2 h-1 (doubling time of 3.5 h) were achieved when Synechococcus 7002 was cultivated in turbidostat mode under light-limited conditions at 30C ... approached a maximum of 0.20 h-1 (3.5 h doubling t
growth_records.notes: Photoautotrophic cyanobacterium; the limiting resource is light (photons), not a dissolved chemical substrate. Reported mu_max = 0.20 h-1, doubling 3.5 h at 30C, Ii=275 umol photons m-2 s-1. Consistency check: td = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.20 = 3.47 h ~ reported 3.5 h. Turbidostat => growth rate equals dilution rate at steady state. Net O2 evolution (surrogate for gross oxygenic photosynthesis) was measured (Clark electrode / oxygraph) and rises linearly with mu, but only plotted in Fig 1B/Fig 2 with no numeric values in text, so O2 rate left null (is_experimental true, value not extractable). CO2 uptake not quantified. Single record captures the reported mu_max condition; the study also spans a continuum of lower mu (~0.05-0.2 h-1) across 4 light regimes with no discrete tabulated rate points.

growth_records.cite: Dethlefsen et al. Differences in codon bias cannot explain differences in translational power among microbes. BMC Bioinformatics (2005). DOI:10.1186/1471-2105-6-3. [PMC546186]
growth_records.notes: Table 1. Temp-corrected-to-37C mu (col3)=0.16 hr-1; actual temp 39C; factor 0.85 (correction downward). Actual mu = 0.16/0.85 = 0.188 hr-1; td=3.68 h. Chemostat (C) so D=mu at steady state=0.188 hr-1. rrn copy# ~ (2). Cited from ref [92]; secondary. No uptake/secretion/medium.

growth_records.medium: Artificial seawater (Instant Ocean based, Sunda & Hardison 2007 trace metals); nutrient-replete

sec.method: particulate carbon loss over scotoperiod, R=2 ln(C12/C0)-D

Continuous photoautotroph; no organic C uptake (inorganic C fixation via photosynthesis, not quantified per gDW). Respiration is a first-order specific rate constant in 1/day, listed as CO2 secretion proxy.
